skill.name: library-detection
skill.description: Detect a project technology stack from package manifests and configuration files, including frameworks, test tools, build tools, databases, and CI signals.
skill.summary: Identify project languages, frameworks, test tools, and build systems from manifest files.
skill.author: Consiliency

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many backtick and command patterns, but review shows they are Markdown examples and local manifest parsing instructions. The skill does direct agents to read project files and run simple local shell commands, so it carries low operational risk and should be used only inside trusted workspaces. No evidence found of prompt injection, malicious network use, credential exfiltration, obfuscation, or weak cryptographic logic.

low_findings.title: Local Shell Commands in Markdown Cookbook
low_findings.description: The cookbook includes shell snippets such as cat, jq, grep, sed, awk, ls, and python3 for reading standard manifest files. This is legitimate for stack detection, but agents should avoid substituting untrusted paths or running the commands outside the intended project workspace.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly present in fenced Markdown blocks and are limited to local manifest parsing. They do not show network transfer, credential access, deletion, or dynamic execution of manifest contents.

low_findings.title: Agent Instruction to Execute Parsing Cookbook
low_findings.description: The skill tells the agent to read and execute the manifest parsing cookbook when parsing manifests. This creates expected tool use, but the referenced cookbook contains only local read and parsing commands for known project files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The execute instruction is direct and should be treated as operationally relevant. Context shows it points to benign local parsing steps rather than arbitrary code or remote payloads.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Network, Credential, Crypto, and Obfuscation Alerts
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL is a JSON Schema identifier in an output example, the device file reference is stderr redirection to /dev/null, and the alleged weak crypto or credential markers are documentation text or file names. No evidence found of network requests, secret collection, obfuscated payloads, or cryptographic operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found these alerts inside Markdown examples and parsing instructions. There is no fetch, curl, credential path access, encryption routine, or hidden command sequence in the reviewed files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell-Based Manifest Parsing Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses shell examples to inspect dependency manifests. This pattern is acceptable for developer tooling, but it should remain restricted to read-only commands and fixed manifest filenames.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands are real examples, but their semantic purpose is read-only parsing of known manifest files. I did not find destructive flags, remote endpoints, or user-controlled command construction.

content.value_statement: New projects often hide their stack across many manifest and configuration files. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code identify languages, frameworks, test tools, build systems, databases, and CI signals.
actual_capabilities: Scans common package manifests including package.json, pyproject.toml, go.mod, Cargo.toml, pubspec.yaml, and CMakeLists.txt.
Extracts production, development, peer, and optional dependency categories when the manifest format supports them.
Maps dependency names to known frameworks, test frameworks, build tools, databases, cloud providers, and CI systems.
Supports JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, Go, Rust, Dart, C, C++, Java, Ruby, and PHP manifest indicators.
Produces a structured stack summary that other onboarding, documentation, and tool configuration workflows can use.
limitations: Detection depends on manifest and configuration files that are present in the workspace.
It may miss frameworks that are vendored, generated, or used without clear dependency names.
It does not verify runtime behavior or prove that every detected dependency is actively used.
Some cookbook steps use local shell tools such as jq, grep, sed, awk, and python3.

use_cases.title: Onboard to an unfamiliar repository
use_cases.description: Create a quick stack inventory before reading source files or changing code.

use_cases.title: Prepare tailored documentation lookup
use_cases.description: Identify frameworks and tools before selecting relevant docs or examples.

use_cases.title: Configure development workflows
use_cases.description: Find test runners, build systems, and dependency managers before setting up automation.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Stack Summary
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the library-detection skill to scan this repository and summarize the main languages, frameworks, build tools, and test tools.

prompt_templates.title: Manifest-Based Onboarding
prompt_templates.prompt: Detect the project stack from available manifests, then explain which files support each conclusion and what a new contributor should inspect next.

prompt_templates.title: Testing and Build Tool Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Use library detection to identify test frameworks, build tools, package managers, and CI configuration. Recommend likely verification steps based only on detected evidence.

prompt_templates.title: Cross-Language Architecture Inventory
prompt_templates.prompt: Scan all supported manifests within the configured depth. Group findings by service or directory, flag uncertain detections, and separate runtime, development, database, cloud, and CI dependencies.

output_examples.input: A TypeScript application with package.json, tsconfig.json, and Vite dependencies.
output_examples.output: Detected TypeScript with React, Vite, and a JavaScript test framework. The summary highlights package.json and tsconfig.json as supporting evidence.

output_examples.input: A Python service with pyproject.toml containing FastAPI, pytest, and SQLAlchemy.
output_examples.output: Detected Python backend using FastAPI, pytest for tests, and SQL database access through SQLAlchemy. The result separates runtime and development dependencies.

output_examples.input: A mixed repository with go.mod, Cargo.toml, and CMakeLists.txt.
output_examples.output: Detected Go, Rust, and C or C++ components. The summary lists framework matches, build systems, and files that require manual confirmation.

best_practices: Run the skill from the repository root so manifest discovery has the right context.
Treat detections as evidence-based signals and confirm important conclusions with source imports or config files.
Keep shell parsing read-only and avoid expanding paths supplied by untrusted project content.
anti_patterns: Do not report a framework only because a related word appears in documentation.
Do not skip lockfiles, workspace files, or nested service directories when the repository is multi-package.
Do not run install, build, or network commands as part of basic stack detection.

faq.question: What files does this skill inspect?
faq.answer: It focuses on package manifests and common configuration files such as package.json, pyproject.toml, go.mod, Cargo.toml, pubspec.yaml, and CMakeLists.txt.

faq.question: Does it install dependencies?
faq.answer: No. The documented workflow is intended for read-only detection and parsing, not dependency installation.

faq.question: Can it detect multiple languages in one repository?
faq.answer: Yes. It can report several languages and toolchains when multiple supported manifests are present.

faq.question: How accurate are framework detections?
faq.answer: They are strongest when a dependency name appears in a manifest. Important results should still be confirmed with imports or configuration files.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use the results in later tasks?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is designed to provide structured stack context for onboarding, documentation lookup, testing, and tool configuration.

faq.question: What security care is needed?
faq.answer: Use it in trusted workspaces and keep parsing commands read-only. Do not let project content change command paths or add network behavior.
